A sequence \(\{a_n\}\) is a list of numbers \(a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots\). The sequence \(\{a_n\}\), where \(a_n = n^2\) begins with the terms 1, 4, 9, ....

A geometric progression is a sequence of the form \(a\), \(ar^2\), \(ar^3\), ....

A set is countable if:

  • it is finite
  • or infinite and there exists a one-to-one correspondence between the set of positive integers and the set.

A set that is not countable is called uncountable.

The following notation represents the sum of the numbers \(a_1\), \(a_2\), ..., \(a_n\):$$\sum^n_{k=1} a_k = a_1 + a_2 + \cdots +a_n$$Here are some properties:$$\begin{align} \sum^n_{j=m} ka_j &= k\sum^n_{j=m} a_j \\ \sum^n_{j=m} (a_j+b_j) &= \sum^n_{j=m} a_j+ \sum^n_{j=m} b_j \\ \sum^n_{i=m}\sum^q_{j=p} (a_ib_j) &= \left( \sum^n_{i=m} a_i \right) \left( \sum^q_{j=p} b_j \right) \\ \sum^n_{i=m}\sum^q_{j=p} (a_ib_j) &= \sum^q_{j=p}\sum^n_{i=m} (a_ib_j) \end{align}$$The product of the numbers \(a_1\), \(a_2\), ..., \(a_n\) is denoted by:$$\prod^n_{j=1} a_j = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n$$If \(n \ge 1\), then \(n!\) (read as "\(n\) factorial") is the product of the integers 1, 2, .., \(n\).

Also, \(0! = 1\).
Conjecture a formula for the \(n\)th term of \(\{a_n\}\), if the first six terms are:$$10,13,16,19,22,25$$
Conjecture a formula for the \(n\)th term of \(\{a_n\}\), if the first six terms are:$$3,6,11,18,27,38$$
Conjecture a formula for the \(n\)th term of \(\{a_n\}\), if the first six terms are:$$1,3,4,7,11,18$$
Is the set of all integers greater than -100 is countable?
Yes, because we can add 101 to all integers in this set to get a set of positive integers.
Prove or disprove that the union of a finite number of countable sets is countable.

Suppose there are \(n\) countable sets, and call them \(S_1\), \(S_2\), ..., \(S_n\). Since they are countable, each element can be mapped to a positive integer in some way, so some sort of ordering is possible.

Let the elements of \(S_j\) be \(x_{ji}\):$$\begin{align} S_1 &= \{x_{11},x_{12},x_{13},\ldots\} \\ S_2 &= \{x_{21},x_{22},x_{23},\ldots\} \\ &\vdots \\ S_n &= \{x_{n1},x_{n2},x_{n3},\ldots\} \end{align}$$

Now form a new mapping, \(f\), for \(S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \cdots \cup S_n\) such that \(x_{ji}\) is mapped to integer \(n(i-1)+j\). The elements will be listed like so:

x_{11}, x_{21}, x_{31}, \ldots, x_{n1}, x_{12}, x_{22}, \ldots, x_{n2}, x_{13}, x_{23}, \ldots, x_{n3}, \ldots

This gives us a one-to-one mapping with \(S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \cdots \cup S_n\) and the positive integers.

There are a 100 operands in each. Let's compare \(100!\) and \(100^{100}\) first. With \(100!\), each successive operand decreases by one, while with \(100^{100}\) all operands are 100 only, so it makes sense to claim:$$ 100! \lt 100^{100} $$With \(2^{100}\), all operands are 2, so it's definitely smaller than \(100^{100}\). All operands expect one in \(100!\) is greater than or equal to 2, so it makes sense to say:$$ 2^{100} \lt 100! \lt 100^{100} $$As for \((50!)^2\), half of the operands here are equal to the operands in \(100!\), while the other half are smaller than them. We also know that 98 operands in \((50!)^2\) are greater than or equal to 2, so it makes sense to say:$$ 2^{100} \lt (50!)^2 \lt 100! \lt 100^{100} $$

Expanding \(\sum^n_{k=1}\frac{1}{k(k+1)}\), we get:

\left[\frac{1}{1} - \frac{1}{1+1}\right] + \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2+1}\right] + \left[\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{3+1}\right] + \cdots + \left[\frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{n+1}\right]

Simplifying:

\frac{1}{1} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{4} + \cdots + \frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{n+1}

There are lots of terms we can cancel out. In the end, we get \(1 - \frac{1}{n+1}\).
